title: Applying leather conditioner and wax to boots
subtitle: Clean, condition, and waterproof a pair of leather boots
excerpt: How to clean, condition, and protect a pair of leather boots: removing surface dirt, applying a nourishing conditioner to replenish oils, buffing to a base protection layer, then applying a wax polish for waterproofing and a surface sheen.
sourceNotes: Benchmade Footwear care guides. Saphir Medaille d'Or product documentation. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cream or liquid product containing oils and emollients that replenish the natural fats in leather. Leather loses oils through use and weathering, becoming stiff and prone to cracking. Conditioner restores flexibility and prevents surface cracking. Applied before polish.

A traditional blend of tallow, beeswax, and fish oil used to waterproof and preserve leather. Dubbin penetrates the leather fibre rather than sitting on the surface, providing deep waterproofing at the cost of some surface shine. Suitable for heavy boots; not for dress shoes.

content.text: Conditioning comes before waterproofing. A
leather conditioner
replenishes the oils stripped out by water and use; only once the leather is nourished does wax or
dubbin
provide waterproofing. Applying wax to dry, unconditioned leather seals surface cracks in without treating the fibre beneath, accelerating the deterioration.
Step 1, remove dirt and old polish

Brush off dry mud and dirt with a stiff brush. Wipe the boot all over with a clean damp cloth to remove any salt deposits from road grit. For accumulated old wax or surface polish, use a small amount of leather cleaner or saddle soap on a damp cloth, working it into a light lather. Wipe off with a clean damp cloth and allow the boots to dry at room temperature for 30 minutes before conditioning. Do not dry near a heat source; direct heat dries out the leather faster than use.
Step 2, apply leather conditioner

Apply a small amount of conditioner (pea-sized for a full boot) to a soft cloth and work it in small circular motions across the entire boot surface, including the welt line and heel counter. The leather should darken slightly as the conditioner penetrates. Allow to absorb for 10 minutes. Buff off any surface residue with a clean cloth; excess conditioner left on the surface attracts dust and prevents the wax from bonding.
Step 3, apply wax or dubbin

For a finish that retains a shine: apply a thin layer of shoe wax with a cloth or stiff brush, covering the full boot. Allow to haze (approximately 5 minutes) then buff to a shine with a clean brush or soft cloth. For maximum waterproofing on work or walking boots: apply dubbin with a cloth, working it into the welt seam and any stitching. Do not buff dubbin to a shine; it works by staying in the fibre, not by forming a surface film.
Step 4, finish the welt and edges

Work a small amount of conditioner or wax into the welt seam (the stitched joint between the upper and the sole) with a stiff brush. The welt is the most vulnerable point for water ingress; neglecting it means the upper separates from the sole at the stitch line within one season of wet use. Buff the sole edge with a leather burnisher if the edge has dried and whitened.
Common mistakes
Applying dubbin to dress leather.
Dubbin permanently darkens and de-glosses the leather. Use it only on matte-finish work boots; never on polished dress shoes.
Conditioning suede or nubuck.
Standard leather conditioner flattens the pile and stains suede. Suede and nubuck need dedicated suede-safe waterproofing sprays only.
Applying wax to wet boots.
Wax sits on the surface of wet leather and does not penetrate. The boots must be dry before conditioning and waxing; never apply wax straight from the rain.
